items.q: What is the Walker circulation and how does ENSO disrupt it?
items.correct: Walker circulation is the east-west tropical Pacific atmospheric loop: trade winds push warm water west, air rises in Indonesia, sinks over Peru. El Nino weakens it, La Nina strengthens it.
items.misconception: El Nino is a current that flows from Peru to Australia.
items.random_wrong: Walker circulation is the rotation of the Earth around the sun.
items.why: Students treat El Nino as a place or current; it's an oscillating state of a coupled ocean-atmosphere system. Walker circulation is the normal state — ENSO refers to its periodic flipping between phases.

items.q: What is the difference between the tundra and the taiga biomes?
items.correct: Tundra is treeless cold biome (too cold and permafrost-dominated for trees), while taiga (boreal forest) is the band of coniferous forest just south of the tundra in subarctic latitudes.
items.misconception: Tundra and taiga are the same biome with different names.
items.random_wrong: Tundra is in the Arctic and taiga is in the Antarctic.
items.why: There is no taiga in the Antarctic because there is no continental land at that latitude. The biomes are distinct (treeless vs forested) — defined by whether the growing season permits trees.

items.q: What is the difference between primary and secondary data in geography fieldwork?
items.correct: Primary data is collected first-hand by the researcher (surveys, measurements, observations); secondary data already exists and is found in sources (census data, maps, published reports).
items.misconception: Primary data is more important and secondary data is less reliable.
items.random_wrong: Primary data is collected in primary schools and secondary data in secondary schools.
items.why: It's about source, not quality. Secondary data (e.g. Census) is often more reliable than small primary surveys. Good fieldwork triangulates both — the distinction is who collected it, not how useful it is.
It's Live births per 1,000 population. “Deaths per 1,000 population” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which DTM stage features high birth and death rates?
items.why: It's Stage 1. “Stage 3” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately what was the world population in late 2022 (in billions)?
items.why: Exact answer: 8. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
